# Clinical Target Discovery Fixture

### Module: Drug target definition
Priority: ★★★
Exam Specificity:
Definition.
Core Exam Claim:
A drug target is a source-backed biological entity whose modulation can change disease-relevant physiology.
Key Definitions:
- Drug target: a biological molecule, pathway component, or cellular process selected for therapeutic modulation.
Exam-Ready Knowledge Synthesis:
The definition must stand alone because it sets the boundary between a disease association and a tractable therapeutic intervention point.
Exam Use:
Use this in short-answer definitions before giving target-selection criteria.
Must Master:
- Define the target.
- State that modulation changes disease-relevant biology.
- Separate association from intervention.

### Module: On-target effect versus off-target effect
Priority: ★★★
Exam Specificity:
Comparison.
Core Exam Claim:
An on-target effect follows from intended modulation of the selected target, whereas an off-target effect follows from unintended interaction with another biological entity.
Key Definitions:
- On-target effect: effect caused by intended target engagement.
- Off-target effect: effect caused by unintended engagement outside the selected target.
Exam-Ready Knowledge Synthesis:
The contrast is protected because it determines whether efficacy, toxicity, or side effects should be attributed to the designed mechanism or to unintended pharmacology.
Exam Use:
Use this to interpret efficacy and safety mechanisms in target-validation or lead-optimisation questions.
Common Error / Trap:
Do not call every adverse effect off-target; some adverse effects can be on-target if they follow from the intended mechanism.
Must Master:
- Define on-target.
- Define off-target.
- Link the distinction to efficacy and toxicity.

### Module: Good drug target criteria
Priority: ★★★
Exam Specificity:
Criteria list.
Core Exam Claim:
A good target is disease-relevant, modifiable, assayable, and sufficiently selective for therapeutic intervention.
Criteria / Components / Steps:
- Disease relevance: evidence links the target to the disease mechanism.
- Modifiability: the target can be increased, inhibited, activated, blocked, or otherwise controlled.
- Assayability: target activity or downstream response can be measured reproducibly.
- Selectivity and safety: modulation should separate therapeutic effect from unacceptable toxicity.
Exam-Ready Knowledge Synthesis:
The criteria must remain as a list because exam questions can ask for three features and then require justification of each feature.
Exam Use:
Use this as a short-answer checklist or as the first paragraph of a case justification.
Must Master:
- State three criteria.
- Explain why each criterion matters.
- Apply criteria to a named target class.

### Module: Why GPCRs are good therapeutic targets
Priority: ★★★
Exam Specificity:
Case justification.
Core Exam Claim:
GPCRs are significant therapeutic targets because they control physiological function, are accessible at the cell surface, can be modulated by multiple ligand modes, and form a diverse receptor family.
Canonical Example:
GPCRs provide a canonical target-class example for linking receptor biology to druggability.
Exam-Ready Knowledge Synthesis:
Their physiological function makes receptor modulation clinically meaningful; their cell-surface accessibility supports ligand access; their modulation modes include agonism, antagonism, partial agonism, and allosteric modulation; and receptor diversity allows different tissues and pathways to be targeted.
Exam Use:
Use this whenever a question asks why GPCRs are therapeutically significant or asks for an example of a good drug-target class.
Must Master:
- Physiological function.
- Cell-surface accessibility.
- Multiple modulation modes.
- Receptor diversity.

### Module: Target validation
Priority: ★★★
Exam Specificity:
Method workflow.
Core Exam Claim:
Target validation tests whether changing a proposed target produces the desired disease-relevant biological effect.
Criteria / Components / Steps:
- Perturb the target genetically, chemically, or biologically.
- Measure a disease-relevant readout.
- Check whether the result supports causality rather than association.
Exam-Ready Knowledge Synthesis:
Validation protects the workflow from selecting targets that correlate with disease but do not control the disease mechanism.
Exam Use:
Use this in project or scenario answers that ask how to justify a proposed target.
Must Master:
- Perturb target.
- Measure relevant readout.
- Interpret causality.

### Module: Assay versus screen
Priority: ★★★
Exam Specificity:
Comparison.
Core Exam Claim:
An assay is a defined measurement of biological or chemical activity, whereas a screen applies an assay across many candidates to identify active hits.
Key Definitions:
- Assay: a controlled measurement system.
- Screen: repeated application of an assay to a candidate set.
Exam-Ready Knowledge Synthesis:
The distinction is protected because exam questions often ask whether the student understands the measurement tool and the discovery operation separately.
Exam Use:
Use this to explain hit identification and to distinguish experimental design from campaign scale.
Must Master:
- Define assay.
- Define screen.
- Link screen quality to assay quality.
